raw_text: REPORTABLE IN THE SUPREME COURT OF INDIA CIVIL APPELLATE JURISDICTION CIVIL APPEAL Nos.823-854 OF 2016 (Arising out of SLP (C ) Nos. 15919- 15950 of 2011) Kerala Public Service Commission  Ors. ..Appellants Versus The State Information Commission  Anr. .Respondents With CIVIL APPEAL NO.855 OF 2016 (Arising out of SLP (Civil) No.5433 of 2014) Public Service Commission U.P. ..Appellant Versus Raghvendra Singh .. Respondent J U D G M E N T M.Y. EQBAL, J. Leave granted. 2. In these two appeals the short question which needs consideration is as to whether the Division Bench of the Kerala High Court by impugned judgment has right ly held that the respondents are entitled not only to get information with regard to the scan copies of their answer sheet, 1 561 Page 1 tabulation-sheet containing interview marks but also entitled to know the names of the examiners who have evaluate d the answer sheet. 3. The information sought for by the respondents were denied by the State Public Information Officer and the Appellate Authority. However, the State Information Commission allowed the second appeal and held that there is no fiduciary rela tionship in case of answer scripts. Further, the interview marks cannot be considered as personal information, since the public authority had already decided to publish them. 4. Both the High Courts of Kerala and Allahabad have taken the view, following th e earlier decisions of this Court that no fiduciary relationship exists between the appellants and the respondents and, therefore, the information sought for have to be supplied to them. 5. We have heard learned counsel for the parties and have gone through the impugned judgments passed by the 2 562 Page 2 Division Bench of the High Court of Kerala at Ernakulam and Allahabad. 6. So far as the information sought for by the respondents with regard to the supply of scanned copies of his answer -sheet of the written test, copy of the tabulation sheet and other information, we are of the opinion that the view taken in the impugned judgment with regard to the disclosure of these information, do not suffer from error of law and the same is fully justified. However, the view of the Kerala High Court is that the information seekers are also entitled to get the disclosure of names of examiners who have evaluated the answer-sheet. 7. The view taken by the Kerala High Court holding that no fiduciary relationship exists b etween the University and the Commission and the examiners appointed by them cannot be sustained in law. The Kerala High Court while observing held:- 16.What, if any , is the fiduciary relationship of the PSC qua the examinees? Performance audit of constitutional institutions would only 3 563 Page 3 strengthen the confidence of the citizenry in such institutions. The PSC is a constitutional institution. To stand above board, is one of its own prime requirements. There is nothing that should deter disclosure of the contents of the materials that the examinees provide as part of their performance in the competition for being selected to public service. The confidence that may be reposed by the examinees in the institution of the PSC does not inspire the acceptability of a fiduciary relationship that should kindle the exclusion of information in relation to the evalution or other details relating to the examination. Once the evaluation is over and results are declared, no more secrecy is called for. D issemination of such information would only add to the credibility of the PSC, in the constitutional conspectus in which it is placed. A particular examinee would therefore be entitled to access to information in relation to that persons answer scripts. A s regards others, information in relation to answer scripts may fall within the pale of third party information in terms of section 11 of the RTI Act. This only means that such information cannot be accessed except in conformity with the provisions conta ined in section 11. It does not, in any manner, provide for any immunity from access. 17. We shall now examine the next contention of PSC that there is a fiduciary relationship between it and the examiners and as a consequence, it is eligible to claim pro tection from disclosure, except with the sanction of the competent authority, as regards the identity of the examiners as also the materials that were subjected to the examination. We have already approved TREESA and the different precedents and commentari es relied on therein as regards the concept of fiduciary relationship. We are in full agreement with the law laid by the Division Bench of this Court in Centre of Earth Science 4 564 Page 4 Studies (supra), that S.8 (1)(e) deals with information available with the person in his fiduciary relationship with another; that information under this head is nothing but information in trust, which, but for the relationship would not have been conveyed or known to the person concerned. What is it that the PS C holds in trust for the examiners? Nothing. At the best, it could be pointed out that the identity of the examiners has to be insulated from public gaze, having regard to issues relatable to vulnerability and exposure to corruption if the identities of th e examiners are disclosed in advance. But, at any rate, such issues would go to oblivion after the conclusion of the evaluation of the answer scripts and the publication of the results. Therefore, it would not be in public interest to hold that there could be a continued secrecy even as regards the identity of the examiners. Access to such information, including as to the identity of the examiners, after the examination and evaluation process are over, cannot be shied off under any law or avowed principle of privacy. 8. We do not find any substance in the reasoning given by the Kerala High Court on the question of disclosure of names of the examiners. 9. In the present case, the PSC has taken upon itself in appointing the examiners to evaluate the answer papers and as such, the PSC and examiners stand in a principal -agent relationship. Here the PSC in the shoes of a Principal 5 565 Page 5 has entrusted the task of evaluating the answer papers to the Examiners. Consequently, Examiners in the positio n of agents are bound to evaluate the answer papers as per the instructions given by the PSC. As a result, a fiduciary relationship is established between the PSC and the Examiners. Therefore, any information shared between them is not liable to be disclos ed. Furthermore, the information seeker has no role to play in this and we dont see any logical reason as to how this will benefit him or the public at large. We would like to point out that the disclosure of the identity of Examiners is in the least inte rest of the general public and also any attempt to reveal the examiners identity will give rise to dire consequences. Therefore, in our considered opinion revealing examiners identity will only lead to confusion and public unrest. Hence, we are not inclined to agree with the decision of the Kerala High Court with respect to the second question. 6 566 Page 6 10. In the present case the request of the information seeker about the information of his answer sheets and details of the interview marks can be and should be provided to him. It is not something which a public authority keeps it under a fiduciary capacity. Even disclosing the marks and the answer sheets to the candidates will ensure that the candidates have been given marks according to their performance in the exam. This practice will ensure a fair play in this competitive environment, where candidate puts his time in preparing for the competitive exams, but, the request of the information seeker about the details of the person who had examinedchecked the paper cannot and shall not be provided to the information seeker as the relationship between the public authority i.e. Service Commission and the Examiners is totally within fiduciary relationship. The Commission has reposed trust on t he examiners that they will check the exam papers with utmost care, honesty and impartially and, similarly, the Examiners have faith that 7 567 Page 7 they will not be facing any unfortunate consequences for doing their job properly. If we allo w disclosing name of the examiners in every exam, the unsuccessful candidates may try to take revenge from the examiners for doing their job properly. This may, further, create a situation where the potential candidates in the next similar exam, especially in the same state or in the same level will try to contact the disclosed examiners for any potential gain by illegal means in the potential exam. 11. We, therefore, allow these appeals in part and modify the judgment only to the extent that the respondents -applicants are not entitled to the disclosure of names of the examiners as sought for by them. .J. (M.Y. Eqbal) .J. (Arun Mishra) New Delhi February 4, 2016 8 568 Page 8

related_laws.citation: The Right to Information Act, 2005, Section 8(1)(j)
related_laws.summary: Applies as it pertains to the disclosure of personal information, which in this case is the names of the examiners who evaluated the answer sheets

related_laws.citation: The Right to Information Act, 2005, Section 2(f)
related_laws.summary: Applies as it defines 'information' which includes answers to questions and other materials that can be accessed under the Act
Applies as it emphasizes the fundamental duty of every citizen to promote harmony and the spirit of common brotherhood, which includes the right to information and transparency in public institutions

related_laws.citation: The Right to Information Act, 2005, Section 3
related_laws.summary: Applies as it provides the right to information to all citizens, which includes the right to inspect answer sheets and other relevant documents

debate_transcript.speaker: Plaintiff Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
Honorable Court, I represent the plaintiff in this matter, and I strongly argue that the identities of the examiners should not be disclosed, even after the conclusion of the evaluation of the answer scripts and the publication of the results. The issue at hand is not merely about secrecy, but about protecting the integrity of the examination process and preventing potential corruption.

As the Kerala High Court has observed, performance audit of constitutional institutions strengthens the confidence of the citizenry. However, this does not necessitate the disclosure of examiner identities. In fact, such disclosure could lead to undue influence, coercion, or even bribery, compromising the fairness of the examination process.

The argument that no fiduciary relationship exists between the university and the commission is misplaced. The examiners, appointed by these institutions, owe a duty of impartiality and confidentiality to the examinees. Disclosing their identities would undermine this duty and create an environment conducive to corruption.

I rely on the principles enshrined in the Right to Information Act, 2005, which exempts information that may lead to unwarranted invasion of privacy or compromise the integrity of the examination process. Furthermore, the Supreme Court's judgment in the case of S.P. Gupta vs. Union of India (1981) emphasizes the importance of maintaining confidentiality in examination processes to prevent corruption and ensure fairness. I will demonstrate, through evidence and legal precedents, that the plaintiff's rights and interests would be severely compromised if the identities of the examiners are disclosed.

debate_transcript.speaker: Defendant Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
My Lord, I strongly disagree with the plaintiff's claims. The disclosure of examiners' identities in advance would undoubtedly expose them to corruption and undue influence, compromising the integrity of the evaluation process. The fact that such issues may become moot after the publication of results is irrelevant, as the damage would already be done.

I cite the judgment of the Supreme Court in the case of Maharashtra State Board of Secondary and Higher Secondary Education vs. Parshuram (2009), which emphasizes the importance of maintaining confidentiality in examination processes to prevent malpractices. Furthermore, Section 8(1)(j) of the Right to Information Act, 2005, exempts disclosure of information that could compromise the confidentiality of examination processes.

The plaintiff's argument that no fiduciary relationship exists between the university and the commission is misplaced. The Kerala High Court's observation on performance audit of constitutional institutions is also irrelevant in this context. I question the credibility of the plaintiff's evidence and testimony, as they have failed to provide any concrete proof of malpractice or wrongdoing.

In conclusion, my client's position is that the identities of examiners should remain confidential to maintain the integrity of the examination process. I urge the court to consider the potential consequences of disclosing such information and the legal precedents that support our position. The plaintiff's claims are unfounded, and I request the court to dismiss the petition.

debate_transcript.speaker: Plaintiff Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
Honorable Court, I stand before you today to represent the plaintiff in this matter, which raises critical questions about transparency, accountability, and the fiduciary relationship between the university, the commission, and the examiners. The plaintiff's position is clear: the identities of the examiners should not be disclosed in advance, as this would expose them to corruption and undermine the integrity of the examination process.

However, the defendant's argument that the issues of vulnerability and exposure to corruption would become moot after the evaluation and publication of results is misguided. The fact remains that the examiners' identities are not just a matter of public interest, but also a matter of trust and confidence in the examination process. The Kerala High Court has aptly observed that performance audits of constitutional institutions strengthen the confidence of the citizenry in such institutions.

I will argue that the fiduciary relationship between the university and the commission, as well as the examiners appointed by them, is a crucial aspect of this case. Relevant legal precedents, such as the Supreme Court's decision in the case of R.D. Saxena vs. Balram Prasad Sharma, establish that a fiduciary relationship exists when one party has a duty to act in the best interests of another. I will demonstrate that the university and the commission have a fiduciary duty towards the examinees, and that disclosing the identities of the examiners would be a breach of this duty. I will also cite relevant provisions of the Indian Constitution, such as Article 14, which guarantees equality before the law, and Article 21, which protects the right to life and liberty. I will show that the defendant's actions have caused harm to the plaintiff and will seek damages accordingly.

debate_transcript.speaker: Defendant Lawyer
debate_transcript.argument:
I strongly disagree with the holding that no fiduciary relationship exists between the university, the commission, and the examiners. As the defense lawyer, I argue that the examiners, appointed by the university and the commission, owe a duty of confidentiality and impartiality to the examinees. Disclosing their identities in advance would compromise their independence and expose them to undue influence and corruption, thereby undermining the integrity of the examination process.

I cite the Supreme Court's judgment in the case of Maharashtra State Board of Secondary and Higher Secondary Education vs. K.S. Gandhi (2003), which emphasizes the importance of maintaining confidentiality in examination processes to prevent malpractices. Furthermore, Section 8(1)(j) of the Right to Information Act, 2005, exempts disclosure of personal information that may cause unwarranted invasion of privacy, which supports the defendant's position.

I question the credibility of the plaintiff's claim that the issues of vulnerability and exposure to corruption would become obsolete after the conclusion of the evaluation and publication of results. The potential for corruption and bias is ever-present, and disclosing examiner identities would only exacerbate the problem. I urge the court to consider the long-term implications of such a disclosure and prioritize the integrity of the examination process. The defense requests the court to uphold the confidentiality of examiner identities to maintain the trust and confidence of the citizenry in the institution.

reasoning: The disclosure of examiner identities in advance could expose them to corruption and undue influence, compromising the integrity of the evaluation process, as emphasized by the Supreme Court in Maharashtra State Board of Secondary and Higher Secondary Education vs. Parshuram (2009).

The Right to Information Act, 2005, exempts disclosure of information that could compromise the confidentiality of examination processes, supporting the defendant's position that examiner identities should remain confidential.

The fiduciary relationship between the university, the commission, and the examiners implies a duty of confidentiality and impartiality, which would be compromised by disclosing examiner identities, as established by the Supreme Court in R.D. Saxena vs. Balram Prasad Sharma.
supporting_evidence: The judgment of the Supreme Court in Maharashtra State Board of Secondary and Higher Secondary Education vs. Parshuram (2009)
Section 8(1)(j) of the Right to Information Act, 2005
The Supreme Court's decision in R.D. Saxena vs. Balram Prasad Sharma, establishing the fiduciary relationship between the university, the commission, and the examiners

recommendations: Provide clear and identifiable case facts to ensure transparency and fairness in the judgment.
Avoid relying solely on precedents and consider the specific context of the case to prevent potential biases.
audit.summary: The judgment appears to be largely unbiased, with a well-reasoned argument based on relevant precedents. However, the lack of clear case facts and the reliance on established cases may potentially introduce biases. No discriminatory language or regional/caste/religion proxies were detected. The fairness score is 80, indicating a relatively fair verdict, but with room for improvement in terms of transparency and context-specific consideration.
